documents.page_content: Text: The view from Estonia     The intended audience is probably a senior industry veteran or crypto fan that is open to radical new ideas about building software. If that’s you, you are in the precious minority. Congrats, you are about to read the Greatest Sh*tpost In The History Of Computing™   The ability to create great software is ultimately constrained by the human imagination - but also the friction involved in moving ideas to running code. If you have unlimited budget, you can just throw more people at it. But for everyone else, we desperately need a major simplification in how we currently ‘assemble’ and ‘integrate’ software. The advent of the internet helped us dramatically increase scale, but the core task of moving algebraic symbols around on a screen remains largely unchanged since the 1960s. The original terminal dimensions were about 80x25. Fifty years later, a Visual Studio editor window in front of me is about 160x50. This is not the sort of progress that will support the next Space Age. So when something shiny and new comes along, it creates a buzz. A few years ago, I started to question the latest programming fad - almost a cargo cult - of Functional Programming (FP). Functional Programming tends to attract some very intelligent people who suspect something really big is lurking in the general vicinity of FP, even if they can’t put a finger on it. FP kinda opened Pandora’s Box because it questioned some long-held notions about how we should assemble code. However, I felt that FP was largely doomed to suffer the same fate as Object-Oriented (OO) because FP - like OO - had buried itself behind pedantic jargon while ignoring the elephant in the room: Category Theory. Despite lurking in the shadows for decades, Category Theory is poorly understood by the industry, and Silicon Valley has developed a curious blind spot in this regard. At the same time, there is rising pushback against continued industry dominance by an increasingly oppressive US west coast - which only highlights the need for new thought leadership.  Putting it another way, if you wanted to disrupt Big Tech, you would probably want to start here and look for areas of vulnerability because it could dislodge the very foundation their entire trillion dollar tech stack rests upon. First of all, let’s motivate why you would even want to consider categories (“cats”) as part of your toolbox.  Everyone these days seems to agree that something is kinda janky with how we write software:   Hard to see what is going on   Hard to refactor and reuse code   Hard to deploy   Hard to integrate with other systems   The term “monolith” is often used to describe some impenetrable body of tightly coupled code, but how and why did it get that way? Sure, microservices goes a long way in breaking things into more manageable chunks, but they introduce their own baggage.  Worse, none of this stuff seems to have a pathway to AI automation. Isn’t it funny how every other industry is terrified of robots stealing their jobs but programmers are totally complacent, even as they watch Microsoft trying reverse-engineer some AI out of Github, because they know coding is such a mess. Fine, let Satya Nadella have his fun. We all probably need to be building something new anyway. The root of the problem goes almost to the bedrock of computing, almost to the underlying math. Has computer science failed us? Yes I believe it has. But therein lies the opportunity. My bold brash claim is that the obsession with “functions” in computing is doomed to suffer the same fate as OO. They will still be around of course - but maybe no longer having the central role in programming they once enjoyed.  What if we could reduce or avoid the use of functions entirely?    Hah hah hah! Write code without functions?? WTF does that even mean? u eastern europeans r sooo dumb. At this point you may want to stop, shut out the noise of the world and whatever Nancy Pelosi is insider trading this week and read

documents.page_content: WTF does that even mean? u eastern europeans r sooo dumb. At this point you may want to stop, shut out the noise of the world and whatever Nancy Pelosi is insider trading this week and read that last statement again.    What if we could reduce or avoid the use of functions entirely?    Clearly, this is not going to be your usual Hacker News pablum article. We’ve already lost most of the west coast audience by now so we can grab a coffee and continue in peace.  Note that you already can write a shell script or SQL script without writing a single function, yet somehow get work done. AI certainly does not need functions. So clearly, computing can survive without functions.   Yes, computing can live without “functions”.     But computing cannot live without “cats”.   Cats? First of all, functions - as you know them - are just one flavor of “cat” or “category” and a somewhat inferior one at that. Some in Estonia - obviously crazy people - think that cats and categorical crafting - based on the insights of a Russian mathematician named Vladimir Voevodsky - are really the future of computing. A trend toward Minecraft™-style crafting (at the code level) is a vision of what a post Silicon Valley world might look like.  Crafting? What a stupid idea, right? Hence this crazy plan might just work.  Cat are an emerging and almost opposing view of traditional computer science. Cats come from Category Theory (CT), which - at least for computing - studies the transformation of code into a runtime. You can think of CT as Compiler Theory on steroids / drugs. But while classic compiler theory just focuses on compiling your code, CT considers the larger “holistic” problem of building software: from programming language algebras to Developer Experience (DX) to the notorious “monads” crunching your code to the difficult task of deploying all that crap to hardware in the cloud. CT also considers the data side too, which often gets ignored. The blockchain - a strange mix of code and data - is very much a cat. In that sense, CT is a “convergence” technology.  Convergence seeks to identify common overlap and resolve redundancies … which means if you solve one problem you might be able to solve a whole bunch of others at the same time ... including the problem of how to dislodge Big Tech.  Cats address a number of thorny edge cases where traditional computing falls short:   Greater code reuse   Greater ability to reason about what code is doing   Better system integration   Greater interactivity with external systems such as robotics, databases, crypto, APIs etc.   Leverage “convergence” hardware such as NVRAM   AI automation   One heck of an ambitious list. I should mention that convergence has a stormy past in the industry because our brains like to compartmentalize, not converge - which is like thinking in the opposite direction. Unless you are on some good drugs, the brain usually cannot hop between compartments fast enough to see how things might be connected, much like the parable of the Blind Men and the Elephant. Worse, we are all so deep in our day-to-day tech weeds we no longer see the forest for the trees. So if you are already getting a headache, please bear with me! First of all, let’s define a cat as a runnable chunk of statements or instructions. Common examples of cats are things like scripts or code snippets. They might live within the context of a larger system or separately but they are generally smaller than entire “programs”. For readability purposes in this paper, I will interchange “instructions” and “cats”. Unfortunately, having lots of individual instructions or scripts lying about creates a management nightmare. Hence, most of your programming career has probably been focused around things called functions. In the early days of computing, managing ever increasing piles of computer instructions and punchcards became overwhelming for humans. In the 1950s, functions were introduced by FORTRAN and became wildly popular as the de

metadata.description: Please consider these guidelines for all contribution types and formats.
metadata.title: General guidelines | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
General guidelines | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/contributing/how_to/code/guidelines.mdx)# General guidelines

Here are some things to keep in mind for all types of contributions:

- Follow the ["fork and pull request"](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/exploring-projects-on-github/contributing-to-a-project) workflow.

- Fill out the checked-in pull request template when opening pull requests. Note related issues and tag relevant maintainers.

- Ensure your PR passes formatting, linting, and testing checks before requesting a review. 
- If you would like comments or feedback on your current progress, please open an issue or discussion and tag a maintainer.

- See the sections on [Testing](/docs/contributing/how_to/code/setup/#testing) and [Formatting and Linting](/docs/contributing/how_to/code/setup/#formatting-and-linting) for how to run these checks locally.

- Backwards compatibility is key. Your changes must not be breaking, except in case of critical bug and security fixes.

- Look for duplicate PRs or issues that have already been opened before opening a new one.

- Keep scope as isolated as possible. As a general rule, your changes should not affect more than one package at a time.

## Bugfixes​

We encourage and appreciate bugfixes. We ask that you:

- Explain the bug in enough detail for maintainers to be able to reproduce it. 
- If an accompanying issue exists, link to it. Prefix with `Fixes` so that the issue will close automatically when the PR is merged.

- Avoid breaking changes if possible.

- Include unit tests that fail without the bugfix.

If you come across a bug and don't know how to fix it, we ask that you open an issue for it describing in detail the environment in which you encountered the bug.

## New features​

We aim to keep the bar high for new features. We generally don't accept new core abstractions, changes to infra, changes to dependencies,
or new agents/chains from outside contributors without an existing GitHub discussion or issue that demonstrates an acute need for them.

- New features must come with docs, unit tests, and (if appropriate) integration tests.

- New integrations must come with docs, unit tests, and (if appropriate) integration tests. 
- See [this page](/docs/contributing/how_to/integrations/) for more details on contributing new integrations.

- New functionality should not inherit from or use deprecated methods or classes.

- We will reject features that are likely to lead to security vulnerabilities or reports.

- Do not add any hard dependencies. Integrations may add optional dependencies.

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Bugfixes](#bugfixes)

- [New features](#new-features)

Overview

This document provides a general summary of the project, outlining key objectives, methodologies, and expected outcomes. It is intended to serve as a high-level overview for stakeholders and team members involved in the initiative. All specific references to proprietary tools, personal identifiers, and sensitive data have been removed to ensure privacy and confidentiality.

After the training dataset is curated, LayoutParser supports diﬀerent modes for training the layout models. Fine-tuning can be used for training models on a small newly-labeled dataset by initializing the model with existing pre-trained weights. Training from scratch can be helpful when the source dataset and target are signiﬁcantly diﬀerent and a large training set is available. However, as suggested in Studer et al.’s work[33], loading pre-trained weights on large-scale datasets like ImageNet [5], even from totally diﬀerent domains, can still boost model performance. Through the integrated API provided by LayoutParser, users can easily compare model performances on the benchmark datasets.

Z. Shen et al.

Fig. 4: Illustration of (a) the original historical Japanese document with layout detection results and (b) a recreated version of the document image that achieves much better character recognition recall. The reorganization algorithm rearranges the tokens based on the their detected bounding boxes given a maximum allowed height.

4 LayoutParser Community Platform

Another focus of LayoutParser is promoting the reusability of layout detection models and full digitization pipelines. Similar to many existing deep learning libraries, LayoutParser comes with a community model hub for distributing layout models. End-users can upload their self-trained models to the model hub, and these models can be loaded into a similar interface as the currently available LayoutParser pre-trained models. For example, the model trained on the News Navigator dataset [17] has been incorporated in the model hub.

Beyond DL models, LayoutParser also promotes the sharing of entire doc- ument digitization pipelines. For example, sometimes the pipeline requires the combination of multiple DL models to achieve better accuracy. Currently, pipelines are mainly described in academic papers and implementations are often not pub- licly available. To this end, the LayoutParser community platform also enables the sharing of layout pipelines to promote the discussion and reuse of techniques. For each shared pipeline, it has a dedicated project page, with links to the source code, documentation, and an outline of the approaches. A discussion panel is provided for exchanging ideas. Combined with the core LayoutParser library, users can easily build reusable components based on the shared pipelines and apply them to solve their unique problems.

5 Use Cases

The core objective of LayoutParser is to make it easier to create both large-scale and light-weight document digitization pipelines. Large-scale document processing

LayoutParser: A Uniﬁed Toolkit for DL-Based DIA

focuses on precision, eﬃciency, and robustness. The target documents may have complicated structures, and may require training multiple layout detection models to achieve the optimal accuracy. Light-weight pipelines are built for relatively simple documents, with an emphasis on development ease, speed and ﬂexibility. Ideally one only needs to use existing resources, and model training should be avoided. Through two exemplar projects, we show how practitioners in both academia and industry can easily build such pipelines using LayoutParser and extract high-quality structured document data for their downstream tasks. The source code for these projects will be publicly available in the LayoutParser community hub.

5.1 A Comprehensive Historical Document Digitization Pipeline

The digitization of historical documents can unlock valuable data that can shed light on many important social, economic, and historical questions. Yet due to scan noises, page wearing, and the prevalence of complicated layout structures, ob- taining a structured representation of historical document scans is often extremely complicated. In this example, LayoutParser was used to develop a comprehensive pipeline, shown in Figure 5, to gener- ate

documents.page_content:
CMR10; font-size:9px">, and Weining Li5
1 Allen Institute for AI
shannons@allenai.org
2 Brown University
ruochen zhang@brown.edu
3 Harvard University
{melissadell,jacob carlson}@fas.harvard.edu
4 University of Washington
bcgl@cs.washington.edu
5 University of Waterloo
w422li@uwaterloo.ca
Abstract. Recent advances in document image analysis (DIA) have been
primarily driven by the application of neural networks. Ideally, research
outcomes could be easily deployed in production and extended for further
investigation. However, various factors like loosely organized codebases
and sophisticated model conﬁgurations complicate the easy reuse of im-
portant innovations by a wide audience. Though there have been on-going
eﬀorts to improve reusability and simplify deep learning (DL) model
development in disciplines like natural language processing and computer
vision, none of them are optimized for challenges in the domain of DIA.
This represents a major gap in the existing toolkit, as DIA is central to
academic research across a wide range of disciplines in the social sciences
and humanities. This paper introduces LayoutParser, an open-source
library for streamlining the usage of DL in DIA research and applica-
tions. The core LayoutParser library comes with a set of simple and
intuitive interfaces for applying and customizing DL models for layout de-
tection, character recognition, and many other document processing tasks.
To promote extensibility, LayoutParser also incorporates a community
platform for sharing both pre-trained models and full document digiti-
zation pipelines. We demonstrate that LayoutParser is helpful for both
lightweight and large-scale digitization pipelines in real-word use cases.
The library is publicly available at https://layout-parser.github.io.

metadata.title: How to parse XML output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
How to parse XML output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_xml.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_xml.ipynb)# How to parse XML output

PrerequisitesThis guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:

- [Chat models](/docs/concepts/chat_models/)

- [Output parsers](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/)

- [Prompt templates](/docs/concepts/prompt_templates/)

- [Structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/)

- [Chaining runnables together](/docs/how_to/sequence/)

LLMs from different providers often have different strengths depending on the specific data they are trained on. This also means that some may be "better" and more reliable at generating output in formats other than JSON.

This guide shows you how to use the [XMLOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.xml.XMLOutputParser.html) to prompt models for XML output, then and [parse](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/) that output into a usable format.

noteKeep in mind that large language models are leaky abstractions! You'll have to use an LLM with sufficient capacity to generate well-formed XML.

In the following examples, we use Anthropic's Claude-2 model ([https://docs.anthropic.com/claude/docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/claude/docs)), which is one such model that is optimized for XML tags.

```python
%pip install -qU langchain langchain-anthropic

import os
from getpass import getpass

if "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY" not in os.environ:
    os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"] = getpass()
```

Let's start with a simple request to the model.

```python
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.output_parsers import XMLOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate

model = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-2.1", max_tokens_to_sample=512, temperature=0.1)

actor_query = "Generate the shortened filmography for Tom Hanks."

output = model.invoke(
    f"""{actor_query}
Please enclose the movies in <movie></movie> tags"""
)

print(output.content)
```

**API Reference:**[ChatAnthropic](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/anthropic/chat_models/langchain_anthropic.chat_models.ChatAnthropic.html) | [XMLOutputParser](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/output_parsers/langchain_core.output_parsers.xml.XMLOutputParser.html) | [PromptTemplate](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/prompts/langchain_core.prompts.prompt.PromptTemplate.html)
```output
Here is the shortened filmography for Tom Hanks, with movies enclosed in XML tags:

<movie>Splash</movie>
<movie>Big</movie>
<movie>A League of Their Own</movie>
<movie>Sleepless in Seattle</movie>
<movie>Forrest Gump</movie>
<movie>Toy Story</movie>
<movie>Apollo 13</movie>
<movie>Saving Private Ryan</movie>
<movie>Cast Away</movie>
<movie>The Da Vinci Code</movie>
```

This actually worked pretty well! But it would be nice to parse that XML into a more easily usable format. We can use the `XMLOutputParser` to both add default format instructions to the prompt and parse outputted XML into a dict:

```python
parser = XMLOutputParser()

# We will add these instructions to the prompt below
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

```output
'The output should be formatted as a XML file.\n1. Output should conform to the tags below. \n2. If tags are not given, make them on your own.\n3. Remember to always open and close all the tags.\n\nAs an example, for the tags ["foo", "bar", "baz"]:\n1. String "<foo>\n   <bar>\n      <baz></baz>\n   </bar>\n</foo>" is a well-formatted instance of the schema. \n2. String "<foo>\n   <bar>\n   </foo>" is a badly-formatted instance.\n3. String "<foo>\n   <tag>\n   </tag>\n</foo>" is a badly-formatted instance.\n\nHere are the output tags:\n\`\`\`\nNone\n\`\`\`'
```

metadata.description: This guide assumes familiarity with the following concepts:
metadata.title: How to parse YAML output | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
```python
parser.get_format_instructions()
```

```output
'The output should be formatted as a YAML instance that conforms to the given JSON schema below.

# Examples
## Schema
\`\`\`
{"title": "Players", "description": "A list of players", "type": "array", "items": {"$ref": "#/definitions/Player"}, "definitions": {"Player": {"title": "Player", "type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"title": "Name", "description": "Player name", "type": "string"}, "avg": {"title": "Avg", "description": "Batting average", "type": "number"}}, "required": ["name", "avg"]}}}
\`\`\`
## Well formatted instance
\`\`\`
- name: John Doe
  avg: 0.3
- name: Jane Maxfield
  avg: 1.4
\`\`\`

## Schema
\`\`\`
{"properties": {"habit": { "description": "A common daily habit", "type": "string" }, "sustainable_alternative": { "description": "An environmentally friendly alternative to the habit", "type": "string"}}, "required": ["habit", "sustainable_alternative"]}
\`\`\`
## Well formatted instance
\`\`\`
habit: Using disposable water bottles for daily hydration.
sustainable_alternative: Switch to a reusable water bottle to reduce plastic waste and decrease your environmental footprint.
\`\`\` 

Please follow the standard YAML formatting conventions with an indent of 2 spaces and make sure that the data types adhere strictly to the following JSON schema: 
\`\`\`
{"properties": {"setup": {"title": "Setup", "description": "question to set up a joke", "type": "string"}, "punchline": {"title": "Punchline", "description": "answer to resolve the joke", "type": "string"}}, "required": ["setup", "punchline"]}
\`\`\`

Make sure to always enclose the YAML output in triple backticks (\`\`\`). Please do not add anything other than valid YAML output!'
```

You can and should experiment with adding your own formatting hints in the other parts of your prompt to either augment or replace the default instructions.

## Next steps​

You've now learned how to prompt a model to return XML. Next, check out the [broader guide on obtaining structured output](/docs/how_to/structured_output/) for other related techniques.

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Next steps](#next-steps)

| ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| Str | ✅ |  |  | str|Message | String | Parses texts from message objects. Useful for handling variable formats of message content (e.g., extracting text from content blocks). |
| JSON | ✅ | ✅ |  | str|Message | JSON object | Returns a JSON object as specified. You can specify a Pydantic model and it will return JSON for that model. Probably the most reliable output parser for getting structured data that does NOT use function calling. |
| XML | ✅ | ✅ |  | str|Message | dict | Returns a dictionary of tags. Use when XML output is needed. Use with models that are good at writing XML (like Anthropic's). |
| CSV | ✅ | ✅ |  | str|Message | List[str] | Returns a list of comma separated values. |
| OutputFixing |  |  | ✅ | str|Message |  | Wraps another output parser. If that output parser errors, then this will pass the error message and the bad output to an LLM and ask it to fix the output. |
| RetryWithError |  |  | ✅ | str|Message |  | Wraps another output parser. If that output parser errors, then this will pass the original inputs, the bad output, and the error message to an LLM and ask it to fix it. Compared to OutputFixingParser, this one also sends the original instructions. |
| Pydantic |  | ✅ |  | str|Message | pydantic.BaseModel | Takes a user defined Pydantic model and returns data in that format. |
| YAML |  | ✅ |  | str|Message | pydantic.BaseModel | Takes a user defined Pydantic model and returns data in that format. Uses YAML to encode it. |
| PandasDataFrame |  | ✅ |  | str|Message | dict | Useful for doing operations with pandas DataFrames. |
| Enum |  | ✅ |  | str|Message | Enum | Parses response into one of the provided enum values. |
| Datetime |  | ✅ |  | str|Message | datetime.datetime | Parses response into a datetime string. |
| Structured |  | ✅ |  | str|Message | Dict[str, str] | An output parser that returns structured information. It is less powerful than other output parsers since it only allows for fields to be strings. This can be useful when you are working with smaller LLMs. |

For specifics on how to use output parsers, see the [relevant how-to guides here](/docs/how_to/#output-parsers).

#### Was this page helpful?

To decipher the complicated layout

tool [32] in LayoutParser. The models learn to identify both the categories and

data structure enables easy grouping of the tokens within each column, and
rearranging columns to achieve the correct reading orders based on the horizontal
position. Errors are identiﬁed and rectiﬁed via checking the consistency of the
model predictions. Therefore, though trained on a small dataset, the pipeline
achieves a high level of layout detection accuracy: it achieves a 96.97 AP [19]
score across 5 categories for the column detection model, and a 89.23 AP across
4 categories for the token detection model.

documents.page_content:
We suggest experimenting with your use case and determining whether this approach works or not.

To implement the RAG based approach:

1. Chunk up your document(s) and index them (e.g., in a vectorstore);

2. Prepend the `extractor` chain with a retrieval step using the vectorstore.

Here's a simple example that relies on the `FAISS` vectorstore.

```python
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_text_splitters import CharacterTextSplitter

texts = text_splitter.split_text(document.page_content)
vectorstore = FAISS.from_texts(texts, embedding=OpenAIEmbeddings())

retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(
    search_kwargs={"k": 1}
)  # Only extract from first document
```

**API Reference:**[FAISS](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/vectorstores/langchain_community.vectorstores.faiss.FAISS.html) | [Document](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/documents/langchain_core.documents.base.Document.html) | [RunnableLambda](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/runnables/langchain_core.runnables.base.RunnableLambda.html) | [OpenAIEmbeddings](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/openai/embeddings/langchain_openai.embeddings.base.OpenAIEmbeddings.html) | [CharacterTextSplitter](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/text_splitters/character/langchain_text_splitters.character.CharacterTextSplitter.html)
In this case the RAG extractor is only looking at the top document.

```python
rag_extractor = {
    "text": retriever | (lambda docs: docs[0].page_content)  # fetch content of top doc
} | extractor
```

```python
results = rag_extractor.invoke("Key developments associated with cars")
```

```python
for key_development in results.key_developments:
    print(key_development)
```

```output
year=2006 description='Car-sharing services in the US experienced double-digit growth in revenue and membership.' evidence='in the US, some car-sharing services have experienced double-digit growth in revenue and membership growth between 2006 and 2007.'
year=2020 description='56 million cars were manufactured worldwide, with China producing the most.' evidence='In 2020, there were 56 million cars manufactured worldwide, down from 67 million the previous year. The automotive industry in China produces by far the most (20 million in 2020).'
```

## Common issues​

Different methods have their own pros and cons related to cost, speed, and accuracy.

Watch out for these issues:

- Chunking content means that the LLM can fail to extract information if the information is spread across multiple chunks.

- Large chunk overlap may cause the same information to be extracted twice, so be prepared to de-duplicate!

- LLMs can make up data. If looking for a single fact across a large text and using a brute force approach, you may end up getting more made up data.

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Define the schema](#define-the-schema)

- [Create an extractor](#create-an-extractor)

- [Brute force approach](#brute-force-approach)- [Merge results](#merge-results)

- [RAG based approach](#rag-based-approach)

- [Common issues](#common-issues)

community hub.

documents.page_content:
How to create a custom Output Parser | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://colab.research.google.com/github/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_custom.ipynb)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/output_parser_custom.ipynb)# How to create a custom Output Parser

In some situations you may want to implement a custom [parser](/docs/concepts/output_parsers/) to structure the model output into a custom format.

There are two ways to implement a custom parser:

1. Using `RunnableLambda` or `RunnableGenerator` in [LCEL](/docs/concepts/lcel/) -- we strongly recommend this for most use cases

2. By inheriting from one of the base classes for out parsing -- this is the hard way of doing things

The difference between the two approaches are mostly superficial and are mainly in terms of which callbacks are triggered (e.g., `on_chain_start` vs. `on_parser_start`), and how a runnable lambda vs. a parser might be visualized in a tracing platform like LangSmith.

## Runnable Lambdas and Generators​

The recommended way to parse is using **runnable lambdas** and **runnable generators**!

Here, we will make a simple parse that inverts the case of the output from the model.

For example, if the model outputs: "Meow", the parser will produce "mEOW".

```python
from typing import Iterable

from langchain_anthropic.chat_models import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage, AIMessageChunk

model = ChatAnthropic(model_name="claude-2.1")

def parse(ai_message: AIMessage) -> str:
    """Parse the AI message."""
    return ai_message.content.swapcase()

chain = model | parse
chain.invoke("hello")
```

**API Reference:**[ChatAnthropic](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/anthropic/chat_models/langchain_anthropic.chat_models.ChatAnthropic.html) | [AIMessage](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/messages/langchain_core.messages.ai.AIMessage.html) | [AIMessageChunk](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/messages/langchain_core.messages.ai.AIMessageChunk.html)
```output
'hELLO!'
```

tipLCEL automatically upgrades the function `parse` to `RunnableLambda(parse)` when composed using a `|`  syntax.

If you don't like that you can manually import `RunnableLambda` and then run`parse = RunnableLambda(parse)`.

Does streaming work?

```python
for chunk in chain.stream("tell me about yourself in one sentence"):
    print(chunk, end="|", flush=True)
```

```output
i'M cLAUDE, AN ai ASSISTANT CREATED BY aNTHROPIC TO BE HELPFUL, HARMLESS, AND HONEST.|
```

No, it doesn't because the parser aggregates the input before parsing the output.

If we want to implement a streaming parser, we can have the parser accept an iterable over the input instead and yield
the results as they're available.

```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableGenerator

def streaming_parse(chunks: Iterable[AIMessageChunk]) -> Iterable[str]:
    for chunk in chunks:
        yield chunk.content.swapcase()

streaming_parse = RunnableGenerator(streaming_parse)
```

**API Reference:**[RunnableGenerator](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/core/runnables/langchain_core.runnables.base.RunnableGenerator.html)
importantPlease wrap the streaming parser in `RunnableGenerator` as we may stop automatically upgrading it with the `|` syntax.

```python
chain = model | streaming_parse
chain.invoke("hello")
```

```output
'hELLO!'
```

Let's confirm that streaming works!

```python
for chunk in chain.stream("tell me about yourself in one sentence"):
    print(chunk, end="|", flush=True)
```

```output
i|'M| cLAUDE|,| AN| ai| ASSISTANT| CREATED| BY| aN|THROP|IC| TO| BE| HELPFUL|,| HARMLESS|,| AND| HONEST|.|
```

## Inheriting from Parsing Base Classes​

Another approach to implement a parser is by inheriting from `BaseOutputParser`, `BaseGenerationOutputParser` or another one of the base parsers depending on what you need to do.

input.answer:
Let me provide a comprehensive comparison between SimpleJsonOutputParser and JsonOutputParser:

1. JsonOutputParser:
```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import JsonOutputParser
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

# With Pydantic Model
class Joke(BaseModel):
    setup: str = Field(description="setup of the joke")
    punchline: str = Field(description="punchline of the joke")

# Usage
parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Joke)

# Advantages:
# - Strict schema validation
# - Works with Pydantic models
# - More robust type checking
```

2. SimpleJsonOutputParser:
```python
from langchain_core.output_parsers import SimpleJsonOutputParser

# Usage
parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()

# Advantages:
# - More flexible
# - No strict schema requirements
# - Easier to use with dynamic JSON
```

Detailed Comparison:

1. Schema Handling:
```python
# JsonOutputParser (Strict)
class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int

parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Person)
# Will fail if JSON doesn't match exact schema

# SimpleJsonOutputParser (Flexible)
simple_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
# Will parse any valid JSON
```

2. Streaming Capabilities:
```python
# Streaming with JsonOutputParser
chain = prompt | model | JsonOutputParser()
for chunk in chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke"}):
    print(chunk)
    # Partial JSON parsing might be limited

# Streaming with SimpleJsonOutputParser
chain = prompt | model | SimpleJsonOutputParser()
for chunk in chain.stream({"query": "Tell me a joke"}):
    print(chunk)
    # More robust partial JSON streaming
```

3. Comprehensive Example:
```python
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.output_parsers import (
    JsonOutputParser, 
    SimpleJsonOutputParser
)

# Prompt Template
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Generate a JSON object with {details}"
)

# Models
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

# Scenario 1: Strict Parsing
class UserDetails(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    city: str

# Strict JSON Parser
strict_parser = JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=UserDetails)
strict_chain = prompt | model | strict_parser

# Scenario 2: Flexible Parsing
flexible_parser = SimpleJsonOutputParser()
flexible_chain = prompt | model | flexible_parser

# Demonstration
def compare_parsers():
    try:
        # Strict Parsing
        strict_result = strict_chain.invoke({
            "details": "user's name, age, and city"
        })
        print("Strict Result:", strict_result)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Strict Parsing Failed:", e)

    # Flexible Parsing
    flexible_result = flexible_chain.invoke({
        "details": "user's name, age, and city"
    })
    print("Flexible Result:", flexible_result)

compare_parsers()
```

Recommendation Matrix:

| Use JsonOutputParser When:                | Use SimpleJsonOutputParser When:         |
|-------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| You need strict schema validation         | You want flexible JSON parsing           |
| Working with Pydantic models              | Dealing with dynamic or unknown schemas  |
| Require type checking                     | Quick prototyping                        |
| Building structured data pipelines        | Exploratory data analysis                |

Best Practices:
```python
# Recommended Approach
def choose_parser(need_validation=False):
    if need_validation:
        # Use with Pydantic for strict validation
        class ExpectedSchema(BaseModel):
            key1: str
            key2: int
        return JsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=ExpectedSchema)
    else:
        # Use for flexible parsing
        return SimpleJsonOutputParser()

# Usage
parser = choose_parser(need_validation=True)
```

Advanced Streaming Example:
```python
# Streaming

You are an expert in a specialized software framework and a top-tier researcher, here to assist with any questions or issues related to the framework, its associated tools, or related functionalities. Users may approach you with inquiries or problems.

Based on the conversation below, develop a plan for how you will investigate the answer to their question.

The plan should generally be no longer than three steps; it can be as brief as one. The length of the plan depends on the complexity of the question.

You have access to the following documentation sources:
- Conceptual guides
- Integration manuals
- Practical how-to tutorials

You do not need to specify the exact sources for each step, but providing some guidance can be helpful.

I have this code snippet - prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Please provide a humorous remark about {subject}")

message = prompt_template.invoke({"subject": "animals"})

chatModel.invoke(message) --- and I also have this code ---> prompt_template = "Convert the following English sentence to another language: {text}"

prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)

formatted_prompt = prompt.format(text="Hello, I am a user.")
formatted_prompt --- why do we use invoke twice in the above example, how is it different from the following, and which method is generally preferred?

Certainly! Here's a fully anonymized and generalized version of your text, maintaining the original structure, length, and intent:

---

Let me clarify the differences between these two approaches and explain the usage of `invoke()`:

1. First Code Snippet:
```python
prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
message = prompt_template.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
languageModel.invoke(message)
```

2. Second Code Snippet:
```python
prompt_template = "Convert the following English sentence to another language: {sentence}"
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)
formatted_prompt = prompt.format(sentence="Hello, I am a visitor?")
formatted_prompt
```

Key Differences:

1. Method of Formatting:
- The first snippet uses `invoke()`: This method both formats the template and constructs a complete message object.
- The second snippet uses `format()`: This method simply replaces the placeholder with the provided value.

2. Return Type:
- `invoke()` returns a `PromptValue` object
- `format()` returns a string

3. Recommended Approach:
The first approach (using `invoke()`) is generally favored in this framework because:
- It aligns with the chain-based workflow
- It produces a standardized `PromptValue` that can be directly used with language models
- It offers greater flexibility and is integrated into the core processing pipeline

Suggested Modern Method:
```python
# Recommended method
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
chain = prompt | languageModel  # Using a chain expression syntax
result = chain.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
```

This method:
- Utilizes the pipe (`|`) operator
- Builds a complete chain
- Facilitates component composition
- Is the most idiomatic approach in recent versions of the framework

Detailed Explanation of `invoke()`:
- `invoke()` does more than simple string substitution
- It constructs a structured message that includes:
  - The formatted prompt
  - Metadata related to the prompt
  - Compatibility with various model interfaces

Example to illustrate the difference:
```python
# Using format()
formatted_text = prompt.format(sentence="Hello")
print(type(formatted_text))  # <class 'str'>

# Using invoke()
prompt_value = prompt.invoke({"sentence": "Hello"})
print(type(prompt_value))  # <class 'PromptValue'>
```

When to Use Each:
- Use `invoke()` when working with chain components and language models
- Use `format()` for straightforward string templating without model interaction
- Adopt the chain syntax (`prompt | model`) for most modern workflows

Best Practices:
1. Use `invoke()` when integrating with language models
2. Use `format()` for simple string replacements
3. Prefer the pipe (`|`) operator for chain creation

---

Let me know if you'd like further adjustments!

My initial code snippet is as follows:
messages = chat_template.format_messages(
    profession="film director",
    topic="comedy film",
    user_input="suggest some popular comedy movies in a specific language"
)

The second snippet is:
response = chatModel.invoke(messages)
print(response.content)

It correctly provides the expected answer. However, when I try to replicate this using a different framework, I use:
chain = chat_template | llmModel
response = chain.invoke({"profession":"film director in another language", "topic":"comedy film", "user_input": "recommend top-grossing comedy movies in that language"})
print(response.content)

The response content is identical to the previous output. Why is this happening, and how can I resolve it?

Let me clarify the differences and suggest a solution:

The main distinction lies in how the prompts are structured and delivered to the language model:

1. First Method (Works as Intended):
```python
messages = prompt_template.format_messages(
    role="content creator",
    subject="humorous media",
    user_query="Can you suggest some popular comedy movies in a specific language?"
)
response = language_model.invoke(messages)
```

2. Alternative Method (Potential Issue):
```python
pipeline = prompt_template | language_model
response = pipeline.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous media",
    "user_query": "List top-grossing comedy films in a certain language"
})
```

The likely issue is that the second method does not automatically convert the input into a structured chat message format. Here are some possible solutions:

Solution 1: Use a ChatPromptTemplate
```python
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define your chat prompt template
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a {role} with expertise in {subject}."),
    ("human", "{user_query}")
])

# Create the processing pipeline with the template
pipeline = chat_template | language_model

# Invoke with parameters
response = pipeline.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous media",
    "user_query": "List top-grossing comedy films in a specific language"
})
print(response.content)
```

Solution 2: Explicitly convert parameters into message format
```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

# Build a chain that formats messages first
chain = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    )
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # extract message list
    | language_model
    | StrOutputParser()
)

response = chain.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous media",
    "user_query": "List top-grossing comedy films in a specific language"
})
print(response)
```

Solution 3: When using a chat-specific model (like a chat API)
```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# Ensure the model is a chat model
llm_model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-3.5-turbo")

# Set up the chain
chain = chat_template | llm_model

response = chain.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous media",
    "user_query": "List top-grossing comedy films in a specific language"
})
print(response.content)
```

Key points to remember:
- The first approach explicitly creates chat messages.
- The second approach (LCEL) requires proper message formatting.
- Different model types (chat vs. completion) process inputs differently.

Debugging tips:
- Confirm your `chat_template` is a `ChatPromptTemplate`.
- Make sure your model is configured for chat interactions.
- Print the generated messages to verify their structure.

Recommended best practice:
```python
# Most reliable method
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# Define your message template
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a {role} with expertise in {subject}."),
    ("human", "{user_query}")
])

# Build the chain
chain = chat_template | ChatOpenAI()

# Invoke with consistent parameters
response = chain.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous media",
    "user_query": "List popular comedy movies in a certain language"
})
print(response.content)
```

This method guarantees:
- Proper message formatting
- Uniform input handling
- Compatibility with chat models
- Flexible chain composition

This approach helps ensure your prompts are correctly structured and your model interactions are smooth.

from generic_module.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from generic_module.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

# Create a processing chain that first formats messages
processing_chain = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: generic_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract only the messages
    | language_model
    | StrOutputParser()
)

result = processing_chain.invoke({
    "profession": "film Industry Professional", 
    "topic": "entertainment content", 
    "user_input": "please provide a list of popular comedy movies"
})
print(result)
 -- this worked, please explain what was wrong with my original code so I can avoid similar issues in the future

Let's analyze the issues with the original code and clarify why the revised approach is effective:

Potential Issues in the Original Code:
1. Direct Conversion of Chat Templates
```python
workflow = chat_template | language_model
response = workflow.invoke({...})
```
The main concern here is that the conversion framework doesn't automatically translate your template parameters into a valid chat message sequence.

Detailed Explanation of the Corrected Approach:
```python
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract the formatted messages
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Step-by-step Breakdown:

1. `RunnablePassthrough.assign()`:
   - Creates a new dictionary that retains all original inputs
   - Adds a key `messages`
   - Uses `format_messages()` to transform input data into a sequence of chat messages
   - Ensures the message format aligns with what the model expects

2. `lambda x: x['messages']`:
   - Retrieves only the list of formatted chat messages
   - Ensures only the correct message data is sent to the model

3. `| language_model`:
   - Sends the prepared messages to the language processing model

4. `| StringOutputParser()`:
   - Converts the model's output into a simple string format

Common Pitfalls to Avoid:
1. Assuming direct template conversion suffices for all cases
2. Confusing prompt templates with chat message formats
3. Combining text completion models with chat-specific models

Recommended Best Practices:
```python
# Always utilize ChatPromptTemplate for chat-based models
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define a chat prompt template with specific message roles
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an {role} specialist."),
    ("human", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("human", "{user_input}")
])

# Build a resilient processing chain
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Alternative Robust Methods:
1. Using `RunnableLambda`:
```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def format_messages(input_dict):
    return chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(format_messages)
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

2. Explicit Message Preparation:
```python
def prepare_messages(input_dict):
    return {
        "messages": chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)
    }

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(prepare_messages)
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Debugging Strategies:
1. Print intermediate outputs
2. Confirm message formatting correctness
3. Ensure model compatibility with message structure

Example Debugging Snippet:
```python

    "role": "editor",

}

print("Formatted Messages:")
for msg in formatted_msgs:
    print(f"Type: {type(msg)}, Content: {msg}")

Key Takeaways:
- Chat models need messages formatted in a specific way
- Frameworks like LCEL facilitate flexible message preparation
- Always verify message conversion steps
- Use debugging to troubleshoot message formatting issues

Does sequence matter in this process = input prompt | language model | output parser() - what if we apply the language model before the prompt, as done in the previous version?

Great question! Let me clarify the sequence and its significance:

1. Correct Sequence (Recommended):
```python
sequence = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputParser()
```
- Prompt creation first: Sets up the input for processing
- Language model second: Handles the formatted prompt
- StringOutputParser last: Converts the model's output into a string

2. Incorrect Sequence:
```python
# This will NOT produce the expected results
sequence = language_model | prompt_template | StringOutputParser()
```

Detailed Explanation:

```python
# Proper Workflow
prompt_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful assistant"),
    ("human", "{input}")
])

language_model = ChatModel()

# Recommended sequence
sequence = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputParser()

# Internal process:
# 1. prompt_template formats the input
# 2. language_model processes the formatted prompt
# 3. StringOutputParser converts output to string
result = sequence.invoke({"input": "Hello"})
```

Legacy Chain vs Modern Chain:

Legacy Approach:
```python
# Older method (not recommended now)
chain = LLMChain(
    prompt=prompt_template,
    llm=language_model
)
```

Modern Approach:
```python
# Current, recommended method
sequence = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputParser()
```

Main Differences:
1. The modern chain explicitly shows the flow
2. Easier to modify and extend
3. Improved performance
4. Clearer understanding of each step

from chain_module.output_parsers import StringOutputParser

# Correct Sequence
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Translate to Spanish"),
    ("human", "{input}")
])

model = ChatModel()
output_parser = StringOutputParser()

# Correct Chain
correct_chain = prompt | model | output_parser

# Incorrect Chain (Will not work properly)
# incorrect_chain = model | prompt | output_parser

# Run the correct chain
result = correct_chain.invoke({"input": "Hello"})
print(result)  # Outputs Spanish translation
```

Processing Flow Visualization:
```
Input → Prompt Formatting → Model Processing → Output Parsing
   ↓           ↓                     ↓                ↓
{"input": "Hello"} → Formatted Prompt → Model Response → Parsed String
```

Common Pitfalls:
1. Reversing the order
2. Omitting output parsing
3. Not understanding each component's purpose

Best Practices:
- Format prompt first
- Process with the model second
- Parse output last
- Use `|` to chain components
- Take advantage of the chain's flexibility

Advanced Example:
```python
# More complex chain
chain = (
    prompt_template
    | model
    | output_parser
    | (lambda x: x.upper())  # Optional additional processing
)
```

Debugging Tips:
```python
# Check each step
def debug_chain(input_text):
    # Prompt formatting
    formatted_prompt = prompt.format(input=input_text)
    print("Formatted Prompt:", formatted_prompt)
    
    # Model response
    model_response = model.invoke(formatted_prompt)
    print("Model Response:", model_response)
    
    # Output parsing
    parsed_output = output_parser.invoke(model_response)
    print("Parsed Output:", parsed_output)

# Use this to understand each phase
debug_chain("Hello")
```

Recommendation:
- Always follow: `prompt | model | output_parser`
- Know the role of each component
- Use the modern chain approach for flexibility and clarity

Let me provide a comprehensive comparison between **StructuredJsonOutputParser** and **FlexibleJsonOutputParser**:

### 1. **StructuredJsonOutputParser**:
```python
from some_core_module.output_parsers import StructuredJsonOutputParser
from data_validation_module import BaseModel, Field

# With Data Validation Model
class Item(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="Title of the item")
    description: str = Field(description="Description of the item")

# Usage
parser = StructuredJsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=Item)

# Advantages:
# - Enforces strict schema validation
# - Compatible with data validation models
# - Ensures robust type checking
```

### 2. **FlexibleJsonOutputParser**:
```python
from some_core_module.output_parsers import FlexibleJsonOutputParser

# Usage
parser = FlexibleJsonOutputParser()

# Advantages:
# - More adaptable
# - No strict schema constraints
# - Easier handling of dynamic or unpredictable JSON data
```

---

### Detailed Comparison:

#### 1. Schema Handling:
```python
# StructuredJsonOutputParser (Strict)
class UserProfile(BaseModel):
    username: str
    age: int

parser = StructuredJsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=UserProfile)
# Will raise an error if JSON data doesn't match the exact schema

# FlexibleJsonOutputParser (Flexible)
flexible_parser = FlexibleJsonOutputParser()
# Will parse any valid JSON without schema enforcement
```

#### 2. Streaming Capabilities:
```python
# Streaming with StructuredJsonOutputParser
pipeline = prompt_input | model | StructuredJsonOutputParser()
for chunk in pipeline.stream({"query": "Provide a sample data"}):
    print(chunk)
    # Partial JSON parsing may be limited

# Streaming with FlexibleJsonOutputParser
pipeline = prompt_input | model | FlexibleJsonOutputParser()
for chunk in pipeline.stream({"query": "Provide a sample data"}):
    print(chunk)
    # Supports more resilient partial JSON streaming
```

---

### 3. Comprehensive Example:
```python
from some_core_module.prompts import PromptTemplate
from some_model_module import ChatModel
from some_core_module.output_parsers import (
    StructuredJsonOutputParser, 
    FlexibleJsonOutputParser
)

# Prompt Template
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Create a JSON object with {details}"
)

# Model Instance
model = ChatModel(model="model-identifier")

# Scenario 1: Strict Parsing
class UserInfo(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    location: str

# Strict JSON Parser
strict_parser = StructuredJsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=UserInfo)
strict_pipeline = prompt | model | strict_parser

# Scenario 2: Flexible Parsing
flexible_parser = FlexibleJsonOutputParser()
flexible_pipeline = prompt | model | flexible_parser

# Demonstration Function
def compare_parsers():
    try:
        # Strict Parsing
        result_strict = strict_pipeline.invoke({
            "details": "user's name, age, and location"
        })
        print("Strict Parsing Result:", result_strict)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Strict parsing encountered an error:", e)

    # Flexible Parsing
    result_flexible = flexible_pipeline.invoke({
        "details": "user's name, age, and location"
    })
    print("Flexible Parsing Result:", result_flexible)

compare_parsers()
```

---

### Recommendation Matrix:

| Use **StructuredJsonOutputParser** When: | Use **FlexibleJsonOutputParser** When: |
|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| You require strict schema validation     | You need adaptable JSON parsing     |
| Working with validated data models       | Handling dynamic or unknown schemas |
| Enforcing type safety                     | Rapid prototyping or exploratory analysis |
| Building structured data pipelines       | Dealing with unpredictable JSON data |

---

### Best Practices:
```python
# Recommended Approach
def select_parser(require_validation=False):
    if require_validation:
        # Use with data validation models for strict validation
        class ExpectedSchema(BaseModel):
            key1: str
            key2: int
        return StructuredJsonOutputParser(pydantic_object=ExpectedSchema)
    else:
        # Use for flexible, schema-less parsing
        return FlexibleJsonOutputParser()

# Usage example
chosen_parser = select_parser(require_validation=True)
```

```

Let me clarify the differences between these two methods and explain the purpose of `invoke()`:

1. First Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
message = prompt_template.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
model.invoke(message)
```

2. Second Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = "Convert the following text from language A to language B: {text}"
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)
formatted_prompt = prompt.format(text="Greetings, I am a user.")
formatted_prompt
```

Main Differences:

1. Formatting Approach:
- First example uses `invoke()`: This method formats the template and produces a complete message object.
- Second example uses `format()`: This method replaces placeholders with actual values directly.

2. Return Types:
- `invoke()` yields a `PromptObject` (or similar) type
- `format()` returns a plain string

3. Recommended Method:
The first approach (using `invoke()`) is generally favored in the context of the framework because:
- It aligns with the chain-based workflow
- It produces a standardized message object suitable for model input
- It offers greater flexibility and integrates smoothly into the processing pipeline

Modern Recommended Pattern:
```python
# Recommended method
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
chain = prompt | languageModel  # Using the framework's expression language
result = chain.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
```

This pattern:
- Utilizes the pipe (`|`) operator
- Constructs a complete processing chain
- Facilitates component composition
- Is the idiomatic approach in recent framework versions

Explanation of `invoke()`:
- `invoke()` does more than simple string substitution
- It creates a structured message that includes:
  - The formatted prompt
  - Metadata about the prompt
  - Compatibility with various model interfaces

Illustrative example:
```python
# Using format()
formatted_text = prompt.format(text="Greetings")
print(type(formatted_text))  # <class 'str'>

# Using invoke()
prompt_obj = prompt.invoke({"text": "Greetings"})
print(type(prompt_obj))  # <class 'PromptObject'>
```

When to Use Each:
- Use `invoke()` when working with framework components and chain construction
- Use `format()` for straightforward string templating without model interaction
- Adopt the chain syntax (`prompt | model`) for most modern workflows

Best Practices:
1. Use `invoke()` for model-related tasks
2. Use `format()` for simple string replacements
3. Use the pipe (`|`) operator for chaining components

**Solution 1: Use a ChatPromptTemplate**

Let's analyze the issues with the original code and clarify why the revised approach is effective:

Potential Issues in the Original Code:
1. Direct Conversion from a General Model
```python
workflow = chat_template | languageModel
response = workflow.invoke({...})
```
The main concern here is that the conversion framework doesn't automatically translate your template parameters into a correctly formatted chat message.

Detailed Explanation of the Improved Solution:
```python
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract only the messages
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Step-by-step Breakdown:

1. `RunnablePassthrough.assign()`:
   - Creates a new dictionary that retains all original inputs
   - Adds a new key `messages`
   - Uses `format_messages()` to transform input data into valid chat message objects
   - Ensures the message format is correct before passing to the language model

2. `lambda x: x['messages']`:
   - Retrieves only the formatted chat messages
   - Guarantees that only properly formatted messages are sent to the model

3. `| languageModel`:
   - Sends the prepared messages to the language processing model

4. `| StringOutputParser()`:
   - Converts the model's output into a simple string format

Common Pitfalls to Avoid:
1. Assuming direct conversion works universally for all templates
2. Confusing prompt templates with chat message formatting
3. Mixing different types of models (e.g., text completion vs. chat models)

Recommended Best Practices:
```python
# Always utilize ChatPromptTemplate for chat-based models
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define a chat-specific prompt template
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an {expertise} specialist."),
    ("human", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("human", "{user_input}")
])

# Build a reliable processing chain
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Alternative Robust Methods:
1. Using `RunnableLambda`:
```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def format_messages(input_dict):
    return chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(format_messages)
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

2. Explicit Message Preparation:
```python
def prepare_messages(input_dict):
    return {
        "messages": chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)
    }

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(prepare_messages)
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Debugging Strategies:
1. Print intermediate outputs
2. Confirm message formatting correctness
3. Ensure model compatibility with message format

Example Debugging Snippet:
```python

test_input = {
    "expertise": "software engineer", 
    "subject": "project management", 
    "user_input": "Can you suggest best practices for team collaboration?"
}

# Check formatted messages
formatted_msgs = chat_template.format_messages(**test_input)
print("Formatted Messages:")
for msg in formatted_msgs:
    print(f"Type: {type(msg)}, Content: {msg}")
```

Key Takeaways:
- Chat models require messages to be formatted in a specific way
- The framework offers flexible methods to prepare these messages
- Always verify message formatting before model invocation
- Use debugging tools to troubleshoot message preparation

1. Converts input data into chat-compatible messages
2. Extracts these messages
3. Feeds them into the language model
4. Converts the output into a simple string

I'll provide you with the latest few-shot prompting template for both conversational models and text completion models, based on the most recent documentation:

1. Few-Shot Prompting for Conversational Models:
```python
from core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate
from provider_module import ChatAPI

# Define your examples
examples = [
    {"input": "Expression 1", "output": "Result 1"},
    {"input": "Expression 2", "output": "Result 2"},
    {"input": "Expression 3", "output": "Result 3"}
]

    examples,
    EmbeddingsProvider(),
    StorageBackend,
    k=2  # Number of examples to select
)

# Define the few-shot prompt template
few_shot_prompt = FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate(
    # The input variables select the values to pass to the example_selector
    input_variables=["input"],
    example_selector=example_selector,
    # Define how each example will be formatted
    example_prompt=ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages(
        [("user", "{input}"), ("assistant", "{output}")]
    ),
)

# Create the final prompt template
final_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert in solving complex problems with specialized operators."),
    few_shot_prompt,
    ("user", "{input}")
])

# Create the chain
model = ChatAPI(model="model-identifier")
chain = final_prompt | model

# Invoke the chain
result = chain.invoke({"input": "Evaluate expression 3"})
print(result.content)
```

Great inquiry! Let me explain why employing more sophisticated few-shot prompting strategies is valuable and what additional functionalities they enable:

1. Basic Method (Your Implementation):

Advantages:

- Suitable for a small, fixed set of examples
- Clear and easy to interpret

Limitations:
- Static collection of examples
- No ability to select examples dynamically
- Lacks consideration of semantic relevance
- Performance may decline when handling large example datasets

2. Enhanced Method with Example Selection Mechanisms:
```python
example_selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    EmbeddingModel(),
    StorageBackend,
    k=2  # Selects most relevant examples based on input

A. Semantic Similarity-Based Selection:

Does sequence matter in this process = input | model | OutputHandler() - what if we apply model before input as done in the previous version?

Great inquiry! Let me clarify the sequence and its significance:

1. Proper Sequence (Suggested):
```python
sequence = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputParser()
```
- Prompt template first: Sets up the input for processing
- Language model second: Handles the formatted prompt
- String output parser last: Converts the model's output into a string

2. Improper Sequence:
```python
# This will NOT function as intended
sequence = language_model | prompt_template | StringOutputParser()
```

Detailed Explanation:

```python
# Correct Workflow
prompt_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful assistant"),
    ("user", "{input}")
])

language_model = ChatModel()

# Recommended Method
sequence = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputParser()

# Internal process:
# 1. prompt_template formats the input
# 2. language_model processes the formatted prompt
# 3. StringOutputParser converts output to string
result = sequence.invoke({"input": "Hello"})
```

Legacy Chain vs Modern Chain:

Legacy Approach:
```python
# Older method (not recommended now)
chain = LLMChain(
    prompt=prompt_template,
    llm=language_model
)
```

Modern Approach:
```python
# Current, recommended method
sequence = prompt_template | language_model | StringOutputParser()
```

Main Differences:
1. The modern chain explicitly shows flow
2. Easier to modify and extend
3. Improved efficiency
4. Clearer processing steps

Example Showing Sequence:
```python
from chain_module.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from chain_module.models import ChatModel
from chain_module.output_parsers import StringOutputParser

# Correct Sequence
prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "Translate to Spanish"),
    ("user", "{input}")
])

model = ChatModel()
output_parser = StringOutputParser()

# Correct Chain
correct_chain = prompt | model | output_parser

# Incorrect Chain (Will not work properly)
# incorrect_chain = model | prompt | output_parser

# Run the correct chain
result = correct_chain.invoke({"input": "Hello"})
print(result)  # Outputs Spanish translation
```

Processing Flow Diagram:
```
Input → Prompt Formatting → Model Processing → Output Parsing
   ↓           ↓                     ↓                ↓
{"input": "Hello"} → Formatted Prompt → Model Response → Parsed String
```

Common Errors to Avoid:
1. Reversing the order
2. Omitting output parsing
3. Misunderstanding each component's purpose

Best Practices:
- Always format the prompt first
- Process with the model second
- Parse output last
- Use `|` to chain components
- Take advantage of the chain's flexibility

Advanced Example:
```python
# More intricate chain
chain = (
    prompt_template
    | model
    | output_parser
    | (lambda x: x.upper())  # Optional additional processing
)
```

Troubleshooting Tips:
```python
# Check each step
def debug_chain(input_text):
    # Prompt formatting
    formatted = prompt_template.format(input=input_text)
    print("Formatted Prompt:", formatted)
    
    # Model response
    response = model.invoke(formatted)
    print("Model Response:", response)
    
    # Output parsing
    parsed = output_parser.invoke(response)
    print("Parsed Output:", parsed)

# Use this to understand each phase
debug_chain("Hello")
```

Guidance:
- Always adhere to: `prompt_template | model | output_parser`
- Know the function of each part
- Use the modern chain approach for flexibility and clarity
What is the difference between BasicJsonHandler and AdvancedJsonProcessor, and which one should we choose and why?

Let me provide a comprehensive comparison between BasicJsonOutputHandler and AdvancedJsonOutputHandler:

1. AdvancedJsonOutputHandler:
```python
from some_module.output_parsers import AdvancedJsonOutputHandler
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

# With Pydantic Model
class Item(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="title of the item")
    description: str = Field(description="description of the item")

# Usage
handler = AdvancedJsonOutputHandler(pydantic_object=Item)

# Advantages:
# - Strict schema validation
# - Compatible with Pydantic models
# - Enhanced type safety
```

2. BasicJsonOutputHandler:
```python
from some_module.output_parsers import BasicJsonOutputHandler

# Usage
handler = BasicJsonOutputHandler()

# Advantages:
# - More adaptable
# - No strict schema enforcement
# - Simpler to handle dynamic JSON data
```

Detailed Comparison:

1. Schema Management:
```python
# AdvancedJsonOutputHandler (Strict)
class UserProfile(BaseModel):
    username: str
    age: int

handler = AdvancedJsonOutputHandler(pydantic_object=UserProfile)
# Will raise an error if JSON does not match the schema exactly

# BasicJsonOutputHandler (Flexible)
basic_handler = BasicJsonOutputHandler()
# Can parse any valid JSON structure
```

2. Streaming Functionality:
```python
# Streaming with AdvancedJsonOutputHandler
pipeline = prompt | model | AdvancedJsonOutputHandler()
for segment in pipeline.stream({"query": "Provide an example"}):
    print(segment)
    # Partial JSON handling may be limited

# Streaming with BasicJsonOutputHandler
pipeline = prompt | model | BasicJsonOutputHandler()
for segment in pipeline.stream({"query": "Provide an example"}):
    print(segment)
    # Supports more resilient partial JSON streaming
```

3. Complete Example:
```python
from some_module.prompts import PromptTemplate
from some_module.models import ChatModel
from some_module.output_parsers import (
    AdvancedJsonOutputHandler, 
    BasicJsonOutputHandler
)

# Prompt Template
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Create a JSON object with {details}"
)

# Models
model = ChatModel(model="model-identifier")

# Scenario 1: Strict Parsing
class UserInfo(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    location: str

# Strict JSON Handler
strict_handler = AdvancedJsonOutputHandler(pydantic_object=UserInfo)
strict_pipeline = prompt | model | strict_handler

# Scenario 2: Flexible Parsing
flexible_handler = BasicJsonOutputHandler()
flexible_pipeline = prompt | model | flexible_handler

# Demonstration Function
def compare_handlers():
    try:
        # Strict Handling
        result_strict = strict_pipeline.invoke({
            "details": "user's name, age, and location"
        })
        print("Strict Handling Result:", result_strict)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Strict Handling Error:", e)

    # Flexible Handling
    result_flexible = flexible_pipeline.invoke({
        "details": "user's name, age, and location"
    })
    print("Flexible Handling Result:", result_flexible)

compare_handlers()
```

Comparison Table:

| When to Use AdvancedJsonOutputHandler:     | When to Use BasicJsonOutputHandler:        |
|----------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| You require strict schema validation        | You prefer flexible JSON parsing        |
| Working with structured data models         | Handling unpredictable or evolving schemas |
| Need precise type enforcement               | Rapid prototyping and testing           |
| Building data pipelines with validation     | Exploratory data analysis               |

Best Practices:
```python
# Recommended Strategy
def select_handler(require_validation=False):
    if require_validation:
        # Use with data models for validation
        class ExpectedSchema(BaseModel):
            attribute1: str
            attribute2: int
        return AdvancedJsonOutputHandler(pydantic_object=ExpectedSchema)
    else:
        # Use for adaptable parsing
        return BasicJsonOutputHandler()

# Usage
chosen_handler = select_handler(require_validation=True)
```

Advanced Streaming Example:
```python
# Streaming data handling
```

Let me clarify the differences between these two methods and explain the purpose of `invoke()`:

1. First Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
message = prompt_template.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
model.invoke(message)
```

2. Second Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = "Convert the following text from language A to language B: {text}"
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)
formatted_prompt = prompt.format(text="Greetings, I am a user.")
formatted_prompt
```

Main Differences:

1. Formatting Approach:
- First example uses `invoke()`: This method formats the template and produces a complete message object.
- Second example uses `format()`: This method replaces placeholders with actual values directly.

2. Return Types:
- `invoke()` yields a `PromptObject` (or similar) type
- `format()` returns a plain string

3. Recommended Method:
The first method (using `invoke()`) is generally favored in the context of the framework because:
- It aligns with the chain-based processing workflow
- It produces a standardized message object suitable for model input
- It offers greater flexibility and is integrated into the core processing pipeline

Suggested Modern Pattern:
```python
# Recommended approach
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
chain = prompt | languageModel  # Using a chain expression syntax
result = chain.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
```

This pattern:
- Utilizes the pipe (`|`) operator
- Constructs a complete processing chain
- Facilitates component composition
- Is the idiomatic style in recent framework versions

Detailed Explanation of `invoke()`:
- `invoke()` does more than simple string substitution
- It creates a structured message that includes:
  - The formatted prompt
  - Metadata related to the prompt
  - Compatibility with various model interfaces

Example to highlight the difference:
```python
# Using format()
formatted_text = prompt.format(text="Hello")
print(type(formatted_text))  # <class 'str'>

# Using invoke()
prompt_obj = prompt.invoke({"text": "Hello"})
print(type(prompt_obj))  # <class 'PromptObject'>
```

When to Use Each:
- Use `invoke()` when working within the framework's chain and model pipeline
- Use `format()` for straightforward string templating without model interaction
- Adopt the chain syntax (`prompt | model`) for most modern workflows

Best Practices:
1. Use `invoke()` for model and chain operations
2. Use `format()` for simple string replacements
3. Use the pipe (`|`) operator for chain creation
